steps.title: Pin down the rhetorical goal
steps.content: The student is introducing "Paradise" to an audience unfamiliar with both the novel and its author. An effective sentence must therefore tell readers what the novel is (genre and subject) AND establish who Baraka is (a credential signaling why this novel matters).

steps.title: Find the choice that does both jobs
steps.content: Choice C names the novel, gives its year, identifies it as a much-praised historical novel about colonial East Africa, and tags Baraka as the 2021 Nobel laureate — covering subject, reception, and authorial credential in one sentence.

steps.title: Eliminate the rest by what they leave out or misuse
steps.content:
• Choice A — Salim Baraka, who wrote Paradise and later was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature, was born in Zanzibar in East Africa and currently lives in the United Kingdom.: focuses on biographical facts about Baraka and never tells the audience what "Paradise" is about. Misses the novel-introduction half of the goal. ✗
  • Choice B — Many readers have singled out Salim Baraka's 1994 book Paradise, a historical novel about colonial East Africa, for praise.: describes the novel but omits the Nobel Prize credential, so an unfamiliar audience gets no signal of the author's stature. Incomplete introduction. ✗
  • Choice D — Paradise is a historical novel about events that occurred in colonial East Africa, Salim Baraka's homeland.: introduces the novel's subject but says nothing about Baraka as an author, and the "homeland" framing is an overreach the notes do not support. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
